name: Brute Force Approach
intuition_and_algorithm:
We are given a string that can contain three characters:  '(' which represents an opening bracket ')' which represents a closing bracket '*' which can represent an opening bracket, a closing bracket, or be an empty string  The idea is to explore every possible way to interpret the '*' characters. At each step, we try all interpretations for '*', and check whether the resulting string is a valid parentheses string.This approach uses recursion (backtracking) to explore all combinations.
 Define a recursive function that takes the current index and the number of open brackets. If the number of open brackets becomes negative at any point, return false (invalid string). If we reach the end of the string:  If the number of open brackets is zero, return true (valid string). Otherwise, return false.   If the current character is '(', increase the open bracket count and call the function recursively. If the character is ')', decrease the open bracket count and call the function recursively. If the character is '*', try all three possibilities:  Ignore it: call the function without changing the open count. Treat it as '(': increase open count and call recursively. Treat it as ')': decrease open count and call recursively.   If any of the recursive calls return true, return true. If none return true, return false.

complexities.time:
O(3 n )   the worst case, every '*' can be replaced with '(', ')' or an empty string.
  For each '*', we have 3 choices, so with k '*' characters, we make 3 k  recursive calls.
  If the input string has length n, and all are '*', the time complexity becomes exponential.
complexities.space:
O(n)  ,This is due to the maximum depth of the recursive call stack.
  At most, there are n recursive calls at any time (one for each character).

name: Optimal Approach
intuition_and_algorithm:
The string contains '(', ')' and '*'. Our task is to decide if it can represent a valid sequence of parentheses. The key point is that '*' can be interpreted in three ways:

As an opening bracket '('
As a closing bracket ')'
As an empty string

  Instead of checking every combination (which is slow), we use a greedy technique that tracks the possible number of unmatched opening brackets:

We maintain a range [minOpen, maxOpen] to represent the minimum and maximum possible open brackets at each point in the string.
If we see a '(', both minOpen and maxOpen increase by 1.
If we see a ')', both minOpen and maxOpen decrease by 1.
If we see '*', it can behave like '(', ')' or be empty:
    
minOpen decreases by 1 (assuming '*'' acts like ')')
maxOpen increases by 1 (assuming '*' acts like '(')


If at any point maxOpen becomes negative, return false â too many closing brackets.
We also make sure minOpen doesnât drop below 0 â because you can't have negative opening brackets.

  If after processing the whole string, minOpen is 0, it means a valid configuration exists.

complexities.time: O(N) , where N is the length of the input string.We traverse the string once in a single pass, updating min and max possible open brackets at each character.
complexities.space:
O(1) ,constant space.
  We use only a few integer variables (minOpen and maxOpen) regardless of the input size.

code.python:
class Solution:

    # Function to check if the string is valid using greedy logic
    def checkValidString(self, s: str) -> bool:

        # Variable to store minimum number of open brackets possible
        min_open = 0

        # Variable to store maximum number of open brackets possible
        max_open = 0

        # Traverse each character in the string
        for char in s:

            # If current character is '('
            if char == '(':
                min_open += 1
                max_open += 1

            # If current character is ')'
            elif char == ')':
                min_open -= 1
                max_open -= 1

            # If current character is '*', it can be '(', ')' or ''
            else:
                 # If '*' acts as ')'
                min_open -= 1    
                # If '*' acts as '('
                max_open += 1      

            # If max_open goes below 0, we have too many unmatched ')'
            if max_open < 0:
                return False

            # min_open should not be negative
            if min_open < 0:
                min_open = 0

        # At the end, all opens must be matched for valid string
        return min_open == 0


# Driver code
if __name__ == "__main__":
    s = input("Enter the string: ")
    sol = Solution()
    if sol.checkValidString(s):
        print("Valid parenthesis string")
    else:
        print("Invalid parenthesis string")
